message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>There are no controlled studies on the role of systemic corticosteroids (CS) in patients with coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19). In the absence of high-quality evidence, understandably the recommendations from various organizations are cautious. Several randomized controlled trials are underway but shall take time to conclude. We therefore undertook a meta-analysis to ascertain the role of CS in the management of critically ill patients with COVID-19.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Data Sources</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Electronic databases, including Pubmed, Cochrane library and Embase, were searched, using the keywords of interest and the PICO search technique, from inception to 12th April 2020.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Study Selection</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Studies highlighting the use of CS in coronavirus infection with severe acute respiratory syndrome (SARS), Middle East Respiratory Syndrome (MERS) and COVID-19 were selected based on pre-determined inclusion criteria.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Data extraction</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Data was extracted into an excel sheet and transferred to comprehensive meta-analysis software version 3, Biostat Inc., Englewood, NJ, USA, for analysis.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Data synthesis</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Five studies with SARS-CoV-2 infection were included in the meta-analysis. The rate ratio (RR) for mortality in patients with SARS-CoV-2 infection was 1.26 (95% CI: 0.96-1.65, I
                    <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                    : 74.46), indicating lack of benefit of CS therapy on mortality in critically ill patients with COVID-19. The RR for mortality on analysis of the three studies that particularly reported on patients with significant pulmonary compromise secondary to SARS-CoV-2 infection was neutral (RR: 0.91, 95% CI: 0.63-1.33, I
                    <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                    : 63.38).
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The use of CS in critically ill patients with COVID-19 did not improve or worsen mortality. Pending further information from controlled studies, CS can be used in critically ill patients with COVID-19 with ‘critical illness related corticosteroid insufficiency’ and moderate to severe ARDS without the risk of increased mortality.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: The role of corticosteroids in the management of critically ill patients with coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19): A meta-analysis
